secsa__001_a.text:
Scientific abstract
Research question: Is faecal calprotectin an accurate diagnostic tool for necrotising enterocolitis (NEC) in infants
with heart defects?
Background: NEC is a serious type of gut disease that can develop in infants born with cardiac defects, it causes
inflammation and injury to the gut mucosa and may be life-threatening. Making an accurate and timely diagnosis of
NEC is a significant clinical challenge. In the early stages of the disease the clinical presentation of NEC is difficult to
identify correctly. An inaccurate diagnosis of NEC results in the withdrawal of an infant's milk feed and unnecessary
administration of antibiotics, exacerbating parental stress in intensive care. Calprotectin is secreted into the lumen in
the early stages of intestinal inflammation, thus elevated faecal calprotectin concentrations are evident in infectious
and inflammatory diseases.
Aim: Assess the performance accuracy of faecal calprotectin to diagnose NEC in infants with cardiac defects
Objectives: Collect faecal samples to measure calprotectin levels before and after cardiac surgery, and at the same
time the medical team are suspicious that NEC may be developing when using curr

secsa__001_b.text:
ent 'Gold Standard' (clinical
features, blood tests and gut X-rays).
Methods: A prospective, multicentre study (Alder Hey, Birmingham, Great Ormond Street and Southampton). Target
sample size n=270 infants
Inclusion: Infants who require cardiac surgery or catheterisation for a major congenital heart defect.
Primary Outcome: Faecal calprotectin concentrations at a confirmed NEC diagnosis when using current 'Gold
Standard'.
All infants recruited to the study will have stool collection (100mg) at baseline (hospital admission), and first stool
post-surgery. The calprotectin will be measured using the commercially available enzyme-linked immunosorbent
assay.
Index test: At the time the medical team suspect the presence of NEC the index diagnostic test (faecal calprotectin)
will be collected to coincide with the reference test (gold standard). The calprotectin measurement collected at the
time point when the medical team suspected NEC will be used to produce the Receiver Operating Characteristic
(ROC) curves to assess the diagnostic ability and accuracy of faecal calprotectin to distinguish between NEC and no
NEC.
At the end of the patient's participation, a NEC diagnosis will be indepen

secsa__001_c.text:
dently adjudicated by the 'blind endpoint
review committee'. Retrospective secondary analysis incorporating the ROC calprotectin cut-off value to explore the
impact of a false NEC diagnosis on the clinical and economic burden of diagnostic error for infants diagnosed with
inconclusive suspected NEC.
Timelines: Total study period: 24 months; Recruitment period: 18 months
Anticipated Impact
Future validation study to assess the impact of adding calprotectin to current NEC diagnostic algorithm to measure a
effectiveness to reduce diagnostic error - suspected NEC. Update international NEC clinical guidelines addition of
calprotectin cut off levels to mitigate an incorrect NEC diagnosis and improve the timeliness and accuracy of making
a NEC diagnosis.
Dissemination:
- End of study lay report to participating families
- Conference presentation and publication in journal

secpesor__001_a.text:
Plain English summary
Our Aims:
1. Monitor levels of a special marker found in poo before and after heart surgery to identify if this marker is higher in
infants who develop gut disease in babies born with heart defects.
2. Assess the impact of this special marker for diagnosing gut disease if added to the current tools available to
doctors.
Background
Babies born with a heart defect often require lifesaving heart surgery. A serious type of gut disease can develop in
babies born with heart defects because the heart is unable to pump enough blood to the gut, stopping it from
working correctly.
It is difficult for doctors to diagnose gut disease in the early stages due to the limitations of the current way it is
diagnosed, which includes clinical features, blood tests and gut X-rays.
This limitation in diagnosing gut disease means the medical team is unsure if gut disease is developing and
incorrectly diagnose the baby as having gut disease. This results in the baby’s milk feed being stopped for many
days and given antibiotics unnecessarily. Repeatedly stopping the baby's milk feeds leads to poor growth and
increases parents’ stress.
We want to measure the amount of a special marker
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
found in babies' poo to see if it relates to gut disease. Adding
this special marker to the current available tests (clinical features, blood tests and gut X-rays) could improve the
accuracy of diagnosing gut disease in babies born with serious heart defects allowing for better patient care.
In our previous small study, we found a relationship between this special marker found in poo with early stages of
gut disease. However, to confirm our results, we need to measure this special marker in many more babies.
Design methods
1: Collect poo samples from babies admitted to the hospital before and after heart surgery. The collected poo
samples will be delivered to the hospital laboratory to extract and measure the amount of this special marker.
2: To assess if this special marker is higher in babies with gut disease, we will collect a poo sample at the same time
the medical team are suspicious that gut disease may be developing when using current tests available (clinical
features, blood tests and gut X-rays).
If this special marker is linked to gut disease it will help doctors to give the correct treatment to babies.
Parent involvement in study delivery
Our Heart Advisory Parent Group
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
will join the medical team as part of our steering group and will advise on all
aspects of the study ensuring that the family and patient are at the forefront of any decision making.
Sharing the results of study
An end of study leaflet will be sent to participating families.

1. Background
Necrotising enterocolitis (NEC) is a serious gut disease that can develop in infants born with cardiac defects, it
causes inflammation and injury to the gut mucosa and may be life-threatening.1 The aetiology of NEC in cardiac
infants is multifactorial, associated with poor cardiac outflow and poorly oxygenated systemic circulation resulting in
suboptimal blood flow to the intestines.2
Making an accurate and timely diagnosis of NEC is a significant clinical challenge.3 The clinical presentation of NEC
is difficult to identify correctly in the early stages of the disease. Of concern, infants who are diagnosed with NEC at
a later stage have worse disease.4 Due to the potentially life-threatening effects of NEC, clinicians who suspect a
baby has NEC but are unable to confirm, temporarily manage infants as if they have NEC as a precautionary
measure.
This leads to infants without NEC having their milk feeds stopped for several days and being given unnecessary
antibiotics. Compared to infants with confirmed NEC, the care of in

secdrp__001_c.text:
fants with suspected NEC is widely variable in
terms of antibiotic regimen chosen, length of antibiotic treatment, and length of time nil-by-mouth - all these
variabilities may adversely contribute to the length of hospital stay.5
We want to study a method to improve the accuracy of diagnosing NEC. We want to measure a protein marker
found in baby's stools that if combined with currently available tests (clinical features, lab tests and abdominal X-
rays)6,7 may improve the timeliness and accuracy of making a NEC diagnosis, crucially at an early stage of gut
disease.
The protein marker is called calprotectin and monitoring levels in baby’s stools before and after heart surgery may
help us to understand its relationship to NEC. Calprotectin is a biomarker that is released by white blood cells and is
present in the gut when it is inflamed and can be measured in stool.8 The amount of calprotectin in the stool may
indicate the level of gut inflammation and the presence of NEC in infants with heart defects.9
The addition of calprotectin measurement to the current routine parameters (clinical features, lab tests and
abdominal X-rays) may improve the timeliness and accuracy of diagnosing

secdrp__001_d.text:
NEC in infants with cardiac defects,
hence improving their care pathway and outcomes.
Why is this research important in terms of improving the health of patients?
1. An inaccurate diagnosis of NEC results in the withdrawal of an infant's milk feed, exacerbating parental stress in
intensive care. Improved nutritional support has been associated with increased survival and a reduction in overall
morbidity in infants with cardiac defects.10
2. An inaccurate diagnosis of NEC results in the unnecessary administration of antibiotics. Antibiotics cause
antibiotic-related diarrhoea, which in turn affects expected growth.11,12
3. An inaccurate diagnosis of NEC results in the inappropriate use of intravenous nutrition as a substitute for enteral
feeds during gut rest. Avoiding intravenous nutrition reduces the risk of line sepsis and liver disease.13
4. An accurate and timely diagnosis of NEC will reduce the length of hospital stay. Infants classified with suspected
stay.5,9
NEC have a longer hospital
5. The current per-patient marginal cost due to NEC is £13,900 and the cost due to feeding difficulties after surgery
is £5,500,14 both of which could be reduced with a timely and accurate diag

secdrp__001_e.text:
nosis of NEC, improving patient care.
Review of existing evidence
Importance to NHS and patients
Congenital heart disease (CHD) is diagnosed in approximately 1 in 100 births.15 Significant CHD that required
intervention in early childhood affected 3.6 per 1000 live births in England 2004-2016.16 The unadjusted crude 30-
day mortality rate amongst 9,586 paediatric cardiac operations undertaken between 2019-2022 was 2.1% but was
highest in the youngest babies.7
The number one priority for the ‘Research priorities in children and adults with congenital heart disease: a James
Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership’: how can damage to the gut during heart surgery in children with heart
defects be minimised to reduce complications?17
Another priority listed in the same Priority Setting Partnership is 'What are the best approaches to feeding in babies
with heart defects, to optimise growth, improve outcomes, and reduce parental distress?'17
In our national prospective study of NEC in 1819 infants who had cardiac surgery in 5 of the 10 UK specialist
paediatric cardiac centres we found 4% had NEC, with this proportion rising to 7.5% amongst neonates under 28
days of age.18
Necrotising E

secdrp__001_f.text:
nterocolitis
Infants with CHD are at high risk of developing NEC due to persistent diastolic flow reversal in the abdominal aortic,
which reduces mesenteric blood flow.19 The aetiology of NEC is multifactorial with the interaction of cardiac surgery,
gut hypoxia, poor mucosal integrity, and disruption of intestinal microbial dysbiosis.20,21
Management of NEC involves supportive care, empirical antibiotic therapy, parenteral nutrition, and bowel rest with
gastric decompression initiated as soon as NEC is suspected.22 The exact duration of bowel rest in infants with non-
surgical NEC is based on disease severity and clinical judgment.23
Diagnostic Limitations of Necrotising Enterocolitis
Interpretation of the clinical picture, including relevant plain abdominal radiographs in infants is challenging and is
one of the reasons for a wide variation in the reported incidence rate of NEC, ranging from 3 to 18%.5 Key markers
of NEC including intestinal dilation, ileus and pneumatosis intestinalis are often absent on abdominal radiographs.25
The clinical presentation of NEC includes lethargy, temperature instability, recurrent apnoea, bradycardia,
hypoglycaemia, and shock. Specific gastroint

secdrp__001_g.text:
estinal symptoms include abdominal distention (70% to 98%), blood per
rectum (79% to 86%), high gastric residuals after feeding (>70%), vomiting (>70%), and diarrhoea (4% to 26%).24
In an audit of 195 neonates at one cardiac centre (Great Ormond Street Hospital) 14% had feeds suspended during
an evaluation for NEC for at least 24 hours but did not have a NEC diagnosis. This clinical path can involve
inappropriate use of intravenous nutrition, unnecessary antibiotics administration and longer hospital stays.5,9 Of
note, optimal nutritional support in infants requiring cardiac surgery has been associated with increased survival and
a reduction in overall morbidity.10
Faecal Calprotectin- Biomarker
Neutrophils are first-line responders of the innate immune system and generally combat infections by
phagocytosis.26 Calprotectin is a neutrophil activation marker and is mainly exhibited in the cytoplasm of neutrophils
and expressed on activated monocytes and macrophages.27 Calprotectin is secreted into the lumen in the early
stages of intestinal inflammation, thus elevated faecal calprotectin concentrations are evident in infectious and
disease.28
inflammatory diseases such as inflammator

secdrp__001_h.text:
y bowel Faecal calprotectin is routinely used as a screening
test in the evaluation of inflammatory gastrointestinal diseases and a faecal calprotectin value above 50μg/g should
prompt review and raised faecal calprotectin of greater than 200μg/g should prompt urgent assessment.29
Faecal calprotectin is used as a diagnostic marker for NEC in preterm infants.30 However, infants with CHD pose a
unique clinical presentation in that their gut may be inflamed from birth due to compromised gut perfusion and
investigation.5
therefore warrant specific Additionally, ischemia-reperfusion injury from cardiac surgery results in
pronounced neutrophil activation followed by a hyperinflammation reaction that drives inflammation in NEC.31
Pilot Study
Our published prospective single-centre observational pilot study monitored the relationship between faecal
calprotectin concentration and gut inflammation in 30 infants admitted with cardiac defects requiring cardiac surgery.
Faecal calprotectin for infants who developed NEC (n=5) had a mean concentration of 3528μg/g (1004SD)
compared with 1339μg/g (1008SD) in patients with suspected NEC (n=7) and 390μg/g (410SD) in those without
NEC (n=18) (P=0.0001

secdrp__001_i.text:
). Infants with suspected NEC had a significantly longer length of hospital stay, on average 18
days longer compared to infants without NEC (P=0.03).9
Outcomes
Research is warranted to establish whether faecal calprotectin can be a useful addition to the diagnostic algorithm
for NEC in infants with heart defects.5,9 Important areas for further work include NEC prognostication, which includes
earlier identification of the need for care escalation and the requirement for surgery.32 Considering the proposed
different disease processes for cardiac infants, it is essential to investigate calprotectin levels independently from
other diseases.5,9,31
2) Research aims and questions
Question: Is faecal calprotectin an accurate diagnostic tool for NEC in infants with heart defects?
Hypothesis: Faecal calprotectin is higher in infants who have NEC than infants who do not have NEC.
Aim 1: Assess the performance accuracy of faecal calprotectin to diagnose NEC in infants with cardiac defects
Objective 1: Collect faecal samples to measure calprotectin levels before and after cardiac surgery, and at the same
time the medical team are suspicious that NEC may be developing when using current tests av

secdrp__001_j.text:
ailable (clinical
features, blood tests and gut X-rays).
Objective 2: Faecal calprotectin levels will be plotted using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves to
assess the ability of calprotectin to distinguish between NEC and no NEC.
Aim 2. Assess the clinical and economic burden of diagnostic error for infants diagnosed with inconclusive
suspected NEC
Objective 3. Retrospective secondary analysis incorporating the ROC calprotectin cut-off value to explore the
impact of a false NEC diagnosis in infants with suspected NEC on patient treatment burdens.
3) Research Plan/ Methods
Study design
A prospective, multicentre, cohort-based study.
Setting
Specialist children’s hospitals undertaking cardiac surgery in England: Great Ormond Street Children’s Hospital,
Alder Hey, Birmingham and Southampton hospitals.
Inclusion Criteria
Infants up to 3 months old, who require admission to a specialist cardiac centre for treatment of major congenital
heart disease. Require cardiac surgery or interventional catheterisation, including babies who are treated with
invasive medical therapies of respiratory support or intravenous vasoactive medications and their pathway will
include a cardiac in

secdrp__001_k.text:
tervention of any type.
Exclusion criteria
Patients will be excluded if they are older than 3 months, do not have major heart disease and do have any
gastrointestinal abnormality including gastroschisis or imperforate anus. Also, patients with neutropenia will be
excluded since calprotectin is not activated when there is neutropenia.
Data Collection
All participants will have calprotectin levels measured pre- and post-cardiac surgery. Calprotectin will also be
collected and measured if the medical team believe NEC is developing/ present using current standard assessment
(clinical assessments, laboratory tests and abdominal radiographic findings). Patients will remain in the study from
admission until discharge to home or 30 days post cardiac surgery whichever is first.
Other data collection: diagnosis (cardiac and non-cardiac conditions), interventions received by the infant (surgery
type and invasive medical treatments), cardio-pulmonary bypass time (min); weights (kg), at birth, admission, and
discharge, Z-scores for weight-for-age and length-for-age.
Primary Outcome
 Faecal calprotectin concentrations at a confirmed NEC diagnosis using current tests available (clinical
features

secdrp__001_l.text:
, blood tests and gut X-rays).
Secondary Outcomes
 Number of days with no enteral feeds – nil by mouth (ascertained at 0600 hours).
 Number of days on 100ml/kg/hour or more of enteral feeds (ascertained at 0600 hours).
 The number of days to re-establish breastfeeding post-cardiac surgery.
 Patient length of stay in intensive care and hospital.
 Number of days on antibiotics.
 Number of days on parenteral (intravenous) nutrition.
 C-Reactive Protein (mg/l) and complete blood count.
 Formal radiological abdominal X-ray results.
 Structured clinical evaluation of the abdomen.
Feeding Protocol: patients in the study will be fed based on local unit clinical guidelines for feeding.33
REDCAP will be used to collect data at each site. Data will be anonymised to allow for data sharing.
Consent
Given that no harm is expected with participation in this observational study, a deferred consent procedure will be
used following processes used in prior PICU research.34 Patients will be enrolled on arrival at the specialist centre
soon after birth, at a time when parents have a huge amount going on. Thereafter, parents will be approached at a
suitable time when their child is stable with

secdrp__001_m.text:
written information about the study. Written informed consent will be
obtained before discharge and if any parent declines consent their wishes will be respected by removal of their
child’s data from the study.
Aim 1. Monitor faecal calprotectin levels before and after heart surgery and assess the performance accuracy of
faecal calprotectin in infants who develop NEC
Objective 1: All infants recruited to the study will have stool collection (100mg) at baseline (hospital admission), and
first stool post-surgery. Stool samples will be collected and stored in plastic test tube containers and directly sent to
hospital clinical laboratory. The calprotectin will be measured using the commercially available enzyme-linked
immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit of Bühlmann Laboratories AG (Switzerland) according to the manufacturer’s
instructions. In brief, monoclonal capture antibodies highly specific to the calprotectin heterodimeric and polymeric
complex are coated on the microliter plate when calprotectin was assayed in a single measurement using DS2
automated ELISA processing system (extended range 30–1,800 μg/g).
A limitation identified from our pilot study is the unpredictability of the inf

secdrp__001_n.text:
ant's bowels opening, therefore for each time
point we will get the first available stool and in some infants, there will be delays in stool arriving.
Faecal calprotectin performance analysis to distinguish between infants who do and do not have NEC: Infants with
suspected NEC will conform with the reference test (gold standard - clinical assessments, laboratory tests and
abdominal radiography, the definition was developed for use in a national audit by a multi-disciplinary group).6,7
Reference test NEC diagnosis is the identification of a clinical syndrome involving features from 1) plus either 2) or
3) or both 2) and 3) as outlined below:
1) Systemic signs - Include temperature instability, apnoea, bradycardia, raised inflammatory markers,
thrombocytopenia, and shock features. In NEC these are present with abdominal and or radiological signs stated
below.
2) Abdominal - include abdominal distension, reduced or absent bowel sounds, larger than normal gastric
aspirates, gastric bleeding, rectal bleeding, abdominal tenderness, or cellulitis.
3) Radiological signs - The abdominal radiograph will be performed in the supine anteroposterior position. The
following radiography findings a

secdrp__001_o.text:
re considered typical for NEC:
 Dilated bowel loops (often asymmetrical in distribution)
 Pneumatosis intestinalis
 Portal venous gas
 Pneumoperitoneum
Grading classification for NEC:
 Mild based on features from the systemic and abdominal signs the child recovers after 5-7 days of
conservative treatment.
 Severe a more serious picture with perforation and peritonitis.
Index test: Faecal calprotectin concentration
At the time the medical team suspect the presence of NEC the index diagnostic test (faecal calprotectin) will be
collected to coincide with the reference test (gold standard- clinical assessments, laboratory tests and abdominal
radiography).
Objective 2:
Establishing calprotectin cut-off values to diagnose NEC: The calprotectin measurement collected at the time point
when the medical team suspected NEC will be used to produce the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves
to assess the diagnostic ability and accuracy of faecal calprotectin to distinguish between NEC and no NEC.
The ROC analysis will provide two outcomes: the diagnostic accuracy of faecal calprotectin and the optimal cut-point
value for faecal calprotectin. The identification of the cut-point val

secdrp__001_p.text:
ue will require a simultaneous assessment of
sensitivity and specificity. A cut-point will be referred to as optimal when the point classifies most of the individuals
correctly – area under curve (AUC).
Adjudication of a NEC diagnosis - 'blind endpoint review committee'
At the end of the patient's participation, a NEC diagnosis will be independently adjudicated by the 'blind endpoint
review committee' consisting of a paediatric surgeon and cardiac intensivist both of whom will not be involved in
patient care, a diagnosis will refer to a consensus-based definition (outlined above) using current gold standard-
clinical assessments, laboratory tests and abdominal radiography.6,7 They will be blinded to faecal calprotectin
values. The blind endpoint review committee will independently adjudicate a diagnosis as either: No NEC or NEC. If
there is a disagreement about the final diagnosis, cases will be reviewed and adjudicated in conjunction with a third
physician with relevant expertise. Prof Nigel Hall will be one of the adjudicators, assigned as a co-applicant. The
second adjudicator will be listed as a collaborator.
Aim 2: Assess the clinical and economic burden of diagnostic error
Ob

secdrp__001_q.text:
jective 3. secondary analysis to retrospectively review infants where the medical team were unable to confidently
confirm or reject NEC due to the limitations of the gold standard and therefore classified infants as having suspected
NEC. Outcome measures to assess patient burden on outcome measures, including the unnecessary use of
antibiotic, number of days nil by mouth, length of stay and intravenous nutrition.
Sample size:
For our study we aim to recruit 300 patients, to be able to estimate an anticipated specificity of 0.80 with 5%
precision, i.e. a threshold for the lower bound 95% CI of 0.75, which we have defined as clinically acceptable. This
calculation assumes the expected rate of NEC cases classified according to the gold standard to be 7%.
Furthermore, given the observational nature of the study and lack of harm to the patient from participation, we expect
few refusals and a low attrition rate (death), therefore assuming a loss to follow-up/drop-out rate of 10%, we expect a
minimum of 270 patients to contribute to our analysis.
Statistical Analysis:
We will describe demographic and clinical characteristics of our sample using mean, standard deviation or median,
interqua

secdrp__001_r.text:
rtile range for continuous data as appropriate and frequency, proportion for categorical data. We will check
the representativeness of our sample by describing available characteristics for those patients who dropped out.
Faecal calprotectin concentration pre and post heart surgery will be described and compared using t tests. Changes
in concentration levels will be derived and described and regression analysis will be used to explore the association
between changes in concentration levels and the timing of the samples e.g. time of second sample relative to
surgery time.
Logistic regression analysis will be used to assess the univariate relationship between calprotectin concentration
(baseline, post-surgery and change) and NEC diagnosis; AUC, sensitivity and specificity will be presented, along
with 95% CIs. Regression analysis will be performed to assess relationship between faecal calprotectin
concentration and other factors such as weight, length of surgery (cardiopulmonary bypass) and antibiotic use.
4. Dissemination, Outputs and anticipated Impact
Outputs:
Our primary outputs will be:
 Ascertain whether there is a relationship between faecal calprotectin levels and necrotisin

define calprotectin cut off levels that are clinically significant and sensitive to distinguish between NEC and
No NEC.
 Assess the impact of the addition of a faecal calprotectin cut-off value to the current gold standard in
diagnosing or ‘ruling out’ NEC in infants who were classified as suspected/ inconclusive NEC, to measure
the patient burden of an erroneous diagnosis (nil by mouth/ antibiotic administration/ length of hospital stay)
Anticipated Impact
 Short term: Although infants and clinical staff will not experience a direct benefit or impact at this stage of the
study, we feel by including this non-invasive intervention to the diagnostic discussion for NEC will continue to
build an understanding of the biomarker and start to change the narrative around the clinical practice (stool
collection) in infants with suspected NEC, which will support the validation follow on study
 Long term: Clinical impact and patient benefit potentially within 3 years. A follow-on study (outlined in more
detail below in the future research section) to implement ‘clinical calprotectin cut off levels for cardiac NEC’
to assess the diagnostic accuracy of calprotectin when ad

secdrp__001_t.text:
ded to current diagnostic algorithm (Gold Standard
- clinical presentation and abdominal X-ray) will mitigate an inconclusive suspected NEC diagnosis,
therefore improving the timeliness and accuracy of making a NEC diagnosis.
Potential Patient Benefit:
 Avoid unnecessary withdrawal of an infant's milk feed, exacerbating parental stress in intensive care –
optimise nutritional intake.
 Improved nutritional support has been associated with increased survival and a reduction in overall morbidity
in infants with cardiac defects.
 Reduce unnecessary administration of antibiotics. Antibiotics cause antibiotic-related diarrhoea, which in turn
affects expected growth and gut dysbiosis.
 Avoid inappropriate use of intravenous nutrition as a substitute for enteral feeds during gut rest. Avoiding
intravenous nutrition reduces the risk of line sepsis
Wider health service impact
 Within 5 years: Update international clinical guidelines for the diagnosis of NEC with the addition of
calprotectin cut off levels to improve the timeliness and accuracy of a NEC diagnosis (Association for
European Paediatric and Congenital Cardiology – working group)

Future research - validation study: A more accurate test to confirm NEC will improve patient outcomes if the
reductions in false positive or false negative results lead to more infants receiving the correct NEC diagnoses
(diagnostic yield) and appropriate treatment (therapeutic yield). The degree to which appropriate treatment can
improve patient outcomes depends on its efficacy (treatment efficacy). Although diagnostic yield generally increases
with accuracy, it is also affected by a doctor’s confidence in the diagnostic test.
Title: Assess the diagnostic accuracy of faecal calprotectin to 'rule out' NEC when added to the current diagnostic
algorithm for NEC in infants with cardiac defects. Study Design: Test-treatment cluster randomised controlled trial.
Include all 12 Paediatric Cardiac Units within the United Kingdom and Ireland.
Funding source: NIHR Health Technology Assessment (HTA) Programme
Methods and analysis 1:1 Randomisation. In this pragmatic clust

secdrp__001_w.text:
er randomised controlled trial, we will randomise the
12 specialist cardiac units into intervention and control groups. The intervention group will add faecal calprotectin to
its current diagnostic algorithm. The control group will test and treat according to current guidelines, which will not
include faecal calprotectin. The medical team will include infants under three months old admitted to a specialist
paediatric cardiac unit requiring cardiac surgery/ catheterisation.
The primary outcome will be the number of infants diagnosed with suspected NEC as opposed to confirmed NEC.
Clinical and economic burden of diagnostic error. Post study assessment of doctors’ confidence to rule out or
confirm NEC. A survey will be circulated to the medical professionals at each site who cared for infants with cardiac
defects. The structured electronic online questionnaire will cover domains such as participant and practice
demographic data, type of employment/practice, level of confidence in managing NEC (knowledge/skills), factors
influencing diagnoses of NEC. A 5-point Likert scale will be used to assess the level of agreement between units
who were randomised to add calprotectin to diagnostic

Please describe how patients and the public have been involved in developing this proposal.
I led two focus groups which consisted of parents whose infants were on the cardiac intensive care unit at Great
Ormond Street (total of 9 parents). Open ended questions were asked around: how important is nutrition to you in
respect to all other cardiac problems; how often has your baby's feed been stopped; are you aware of the gut
complications associated with cardiac problems.
Parent focus group outcomes:
- All parents rated nutrition as being as important as medical intervention. Furthermore, parents clearly linked
nutrition and growth with overall wellbeing in relation to cardiac health and convalescents, and all had experience of
feeds being withdrawn for one reason or another. However, the vast majority of parents were not aware of the
implications their child’s cardiac defect had on their gut.
- Deferred consent and appropriate time for researchers to approach families to discus study. Interestingly, parents
felt it was acceptable to be approached soon after their baby was admitted (24-48hrs) as most parents received an
antenatal diagnosis of their baby's cardiac defects and therefor

secppi__001_b.text:
e although a stressful time post-delivery felt
- Tube feeding – parents thought it was important to know how long it would be until they could breast/ bottle feed
their child. Length of time to botte/ breast fed post-surgery has been included to study.
- Parents used the term 'starving my baby', when feed are withdrawn for medical investigations/ gut rest, which
provided further insight into how emotive feeding is for parents.
- Medical terminology - gut disease was perceived to be an acceptable term to describe necrotising enterocolitis
Please describe the ways in which patients and the public will be actively involved in the proposed research,
including any training and support provided.
PPI Team

define calprotectin cut off levels that are clinically significant and sensitive to distinguish between NEC and
No NEC.
 Assess the impact of the addition of a faecal calprotectin cut-off value to the current gold standard in
diagnosing or ‘ruling out’ NEC in infants who were classified as suspected/ inconclusive NEC, to measure
the patient burden of an erroneous diagnosis (nil by mouth/ antibiotic administration/ length of hospital stay)
Anticipated Impact
 Short term: Although infants and clinical staff will not experience a direct benefit or impact at this stage of the
study, we feel by including this non-invasive intervention to the diagnostic discussion for NEC will continue to
build an understanding of the biomarker and start to change the narrative around the clinical practice (stool
collection) in infants with suspected NEC, which will support the validation follow on study
 Long term: Clinical impact and patient benefit potentially within 3 years. A follow-on study (outlined in more
detail below in the future research section) to implement ‘clinical calprotectin cut off levels for cardiac NEC’
to assess the diagnostic accuracy of calprotectin when ad

ded to current diagnostic algorithm (Gold Standard
- clinical presentation and abdominal X-ray) will mitigate an inconclusive suspected NEC diagnosis,
therefore improving the timeliness and accuracy of making a NEC diagnosis.
Potential Patient Benefit:
 Avoid unnecessary withdrawal of an infant's milk feed, exacerbating parental stress in intensive care –
optimise nutritional intake.
 Improved nutritional support has been associated with increased survival and a reduction in overall morbidity
in infants with cardiac defects.
 Reduce unnecessary administration of antibiotics. Antibiotics cause antibiotic-related diarrhoea, which in turn
affects expected growth and gut dysbiosis.
 Avoid inappropriate use of intravenous nutrition as a substitute for enteral feeds during gut rest. Avoiding
intravenous nutrition reduces the risk of line sepsis
Wider health service impact
 Within 5 years: Update international clinical guidelines for the diagnosis of NEC with the addition of
calprotectin cut off levels to improve the timeliness and accuracy of a NEC diagnosis (Association for
European Paediatric and Congenital Cardiology – working group)

prompt review and raised faecal calprotectin of greater than 200μg/g should prompt urgent assessment.29

unique clinical presentation in that their gut may be inflamed from birth due to compromised gut perfusion and

therefore warrant specific Additionally, ischemia-reperfusion injury from cardiac surgery results in
pronounced neutrophil activation followed by a hyperinflammation reaction that drives inflammation in NEC.31
Pilot Study
Our published prospective single-centre observational pilot study monitored the relationship between faecal

Faecal calprotectin for infants who developed NEC (n=5) had a mean concentration of 3528μg/g (1004SD)

g enterocolitis and
define calprotectin cut off levels that are clinically significant and sensitive to distinguish between NEC and
No NEC.
 Assess the impact of the addition of a faecal calprotectin cut-off value to the current gold standard in
diagnosing or ‘ruling out’ NEC in infants who were classified as suspected/ inconclusive NEC, to measure
the patient burden of an erroneous diagnosis (nil by mouth/ antibiotic administration/ length of hospital stay)
Anticipated Impact
 Short term: Although infants and clinical staff will not experience a direct benefit or impact at this stage of the
study, we feel by including this non-invasive intervention to the diagnostic discussion for NEC will continue to
build an understanding of the biomarker and start to change the narrative around the clinical practice (stool
collection) in infants with suspected NEC, which will support the validation follow on study
 Long term: Clinical impact and patient benefit potentially within 3 years. A follow-on study (outlined in more
detail below in the future research section) to implement ‘clinical calprotectin cut off levels for cardiac NEC’
to assess the diagnostic accuracy of calprotectin when ad

ded to current diagnostic algorithm (Gold Standard
- clinical presentation and abdominal X-ray) will mitigate an inconclusive suspected NEC diagnosis,
therefore improving the timeliness and accuracy of making a NEC diagnosis.
Potential Patient Benefit:
 Avoid unnecessary withdrawal of an infant's milk feed, exacerbating parental stress in intensive care –
optimise nutritional intake.
 Improved nutritional support has been associated with increased survival and a reduction in overall morbidity
in infants with cardiac defects.
 Reduce unnecessary administration of antibiotics. Antibiotics cause antibiotic-related diarrhoea, which in turn
affects expected growth and gut dysbiosis.
 Avoid inappropriate use of intravenous nutrition as a substitute for enteral feeds during gut rest. Avoiding
intravenous nutrition reduces the risk of line sepsis
Wider health service impact
 Within 5 years: Update international clinical guidelines for the diagnosis of NEC with the addition of
calprotectin cut off levels to improve the timeliness and accuracy of a NEC diagnosis (Association for
European Paediatric and Congenital Cardiology – working group)

evidence.text:
Plain English summary
Our Aims:
1. Monitor levels of a special marker found in poo before and after heart surgery to identify if this marker is higher in
infants who develop gut disease in babies born with heart defects.
2. Assess the impact of this special marker for diagnosing gut disease if added to the current tools available to
doctors.
Background
Babies born with a heart defect often require lifesaving heart surgery. A serious type of gut disease can develop in
babies born with heart defects because the heart is unable to pump enough blood to the gut, stopping it from
working correctly.
It is difficult for doctors to diagnose gut disease in the early stages due to the limitations of the current way it is
diagnosed, which includes clinical features, blood tests and gut X-rays.
This limitation in diagnosing gut disease means the medical team is unsure if gut disease is developing and
incorrectly diagnose the baby as having gut disease. This results in the baby’s milk feed being stopped for many
days and given antibiotics unnecessarily. Repeatedly stopping the baby's milk feeds leads to poor growth and
increases parents’ stress.
We want to measure the amount of a special marker

found in babies' poo to see if it relates to gut disease. Adding
this special marker to the current available tests (clinical features, blood tests and gut X-rays) could improve the
accuracy of diagnosing gut disease in babies born with serious heart defects allowing for better patient care.
In our previous small study, we found a relationship between this special marker found in poo with early stages of
gut disease. However, to confirm our results, we need to measure this special marker in many more babies.
Design methods
1: Collect poo samples from babies admitted to the hospital before and after heart surgery. The collected poo
samples will be delivered to the hospital laboratory to extract and measure the amount of this special marker.
2: To assess if this special marker is higher in babies with gut disease, we will collect a poo sample at the same time
the medical team are suspicious that gut disease may be developing when using current tests available (clinical
features, blood tests and gut X-rays).
If this special marker is linked to gut disease it will help doctors to give the correct treatment to babies.

Review of existing evidence
Importance to NHS and patients
Congenital heart disease (CHD) is diagnosed in approximately 1 in 100 births.15 Significant CHD that required
intervention in early childhood affected 3.6 per 1000 live births in England 2004-2016.16 The unadjusted crude 30-
day mortality rate amongst 9,586 paediatric cardiac operations undertaken between 2019-2022 was 2.1% but was
highest in the youngest babies.7
The number one priority for the ‘Research priorities in children and adults with congenital heart disease: a James
Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership’: how can damage to the gut during heart surgery in children with heart
defects be minimised to reduce complications?17
Another priority listed in the same Priority Setting Partnership is 'What are the best approaches to feeding in babies
with heart defects, to optimise growth, improve outcomes, and reduce parental distress?'17
In our national prospective study of NEC in 1819 infants who had cardiac surgery in 5 of the 10 UK specialist
paediatric cardiac centres we found 4% had NEC, with this proportion rising to 7.5% amongst neonates under 28
days of age.18
Necrotising E

nterocolitis
Infants with CHD are at high risk of developing NEC due to persistent diastolic flow reversal in the abdominal aortic,
which reduces mesenteric blood flow.19 The aetiology of NEC is multifactorial with the interaction of cardiac surgery,
gut hypoxia, poor mucosal integrity, and disruption of intestinal microbial dysbiosis.20,21
Management of NEC involves supportive care, empirical antibiotic therapy, parenteral nutrition, and bowel rest with
gastric decompression initiated as soon as NEC is suspected.22 The exact duration of bowel rest in infants with non-
surgical NEC is based on disease severity and clinical judgment.23
Diagnostic Limitations of Necrotising Enterocolitis
Interpretation of the clinical picture, including relevant plain abdominal radiographs in infants is challenging and is
one of the reasons for a wide variation in the reported incidence rate of NEC, ranging from 3 to 18%.5 Key markers
of NEC including intestinal dilation, ileus and pneumatosis intestinalis are often absent on abdominal radiographs.25
The clinical presentation of NEC includes lethargy, temperature instability, recurrent apnoea, bradycardia,
hypoglycaemia, and shock. Specific gastroint

estinal symptoms include abdominal distention (70% to 98%), blood per
rectum (79% to 86%), high gastric residuals after feeding (>70%), vomiting (>70%), and diarrhoea (4% to 26%).24
In an audit of 195 neonates at one cardiac centre (Great Ormond Street Hospital) 14% had feeds suspended during
an evaluation for NEC for at least 24 hours but did not have a NEC diagnosis. This clinical path can involve
inappropriate use of intravenous nutrition, unnecessary antibiotics administration and longer hospital stays.5,9 Of
note, optimal nutritional support in infants requiring cardiac surgery has been associated with increased survival and
a reduction in overall morbidity.10
Faecal Calprotectin- Biomarker
Neutrophils are first-line responders of the innate immune system and generally combat infections by
phagocytosis.26 Calprotectin is a neutrophil activation marker and is mainly exhibited in the cytoplasm of neutrophils
and expressed on activated monocytes and macrophages.27 Calprotectin is secreted into the lumen in the early
stages of intestinal inflammation, thus elevated faecal calprotectin concentrations are evident in infectious and
disease.28

evidence.text:
Plain English summary
Our Aims:
1. Monitor levels of a special marker found in poo before and after heart surgery to identify if this marker is higher in
infants who develop gut disease in babies born with heart defects.
2. Assess the impact of this special marker for diagnosing gut disease if added to the current tools available to
doctors.
Background
Babies born with a heart defect often require lifesaving heart surgery. A serious type of gut disease can develop in
babies born with heart defects because the heart is unable to pump enough blood to the gut, stopping it from
working correctly.
It is difficult for doctors to diagnose gut disease in the early stages due to the limitations of the current way it is
diagnosed, which includes clinical features, blood tests and gut X-rays.
This limitation in diagnosing gut disease means the medical team is unsure if gut disease is developing and
incorrectly diagnose the baby as having gut disease. This results in the baby’s milk feed being stopped for many
days and given antibiotics unnecessarily. Repeatedly stopping the baby's milk feeds leads to poor growth and
increases parents’ stress.
We want to measure the amount of a special marker

evidence.text:
nosis of NEC, improving patient care.
Review of existing evidence
Importance to NHS and patients
Congenital heart disease (CHD) is diagnosed in approximately 1 in 100 births.15 Significant CHD that required
intervention in early childhood affected 3.6 per 1000 live births in England 2004-2016.16 The unadjusted crude 30-
day mortality rate amongst 9,586 paediatric cardiac operations undertaken between 2019-2022 was 2.1% but was
highest in the youngest babies.7
The number one priority for the ‘Research priorities in children and adults with congenital heart disease: a James
Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership’: how can damage to the gut during heart surgery in children with heart
defects be minimised to reduce complications?17
Another priority listed in the same Priority Setting Partnership is 'What are the best approaches to feeding in babies
with heart defects, to optimise growth, improve outcomes, and reduce parental distress?'17
In our national prospective study of NEC in 1819 infants who had cardiac surgery in 5 of the 10 UK specialist
paediatric cardiac centres we found 4% had NEC, with this proportion rising to 7.5% amongst neonates under 28
days of age.18
Necrotising E

nterocolitis
Infants with CHD are at high risk of developing NEC due to persistent diastolic flow reversal in the abdominal aortic,
which reduces mesenteric blood flow.19 The aetiology of NEC is multifactorial with the interaction of cardiac surgery,
gut hypoxia, poor mucosal integrity, and disruption of intestinal microbial dysbiosis.20,21
Management of NEC involves supportive care, empirical antibiotic therapy, parenteral nutrition, and bowel rest with
gastric decompression initiated as soon as NEC is suspected.22 The exact duration of bowel rest in infants with non-
surgical NEC is based on disease severity and clinical judgment.23
Diagnostic Limitations of Necrotising Enterocolitis
Interpretation of the clinical picture, including relevant plain abdominal radiographs in infants is challenging and is
one of the reasons for a wide variation in the reported incidence rate of NEC, ranging from 3 to 18%.5 Key markers
of NEC including intestinal dilation, ileus and pneumatosis intestinalis are often absent on abdominal radiographs.25
The clinical presentation of NEC includes lethargy, temperature instability, recurrent apnoea, bradycardia,
hypoglycaemia, and shock. Specific gastroint

estinal symptoms include abdominal distention (70% to 98%), blood per
rectum (79% to 86%), high gastric residuals after feeding (>70%), vomiting (>70%), and diarrhoea (4% to 26%).24
In an audit of 195 neonates at one cardiac centre (Great Ormond Street Hospital) 14% had feeds suspended during
an evaluation for NEC for at least 24 hours but did not have a NEC diagnosis. This clinical path can involve
inappropriate use of intravenous nutrition, unnecessary antibiotics administration and longer hospital stays.5,9 Of
note, optimal nutritional support in infants requiring cardiac surgery has been associated with increased survival and
a reduction in overall morbidity.10
Faecal Calprotectin- Biomarker
Neutrophils are first-line responders of the innate immune system and generally combat infections by
phagocytosis.26 Calprotectin is a neutrophil activation marker and is mainly exhibited in the cytoplasm of neutrophils
and expressed on activated monocytes and macrophages.27 Calprotectin is secreted into the lumen in the early
stages of intestinal inflammation, thus elevated faecal calprotectin concentrations are evident in infectious and
disease.28

Scientific abstract
Research question: Is faecal calprotectin an accurate diagnostic tool for necrotising enterocolitis (NEC) in infants
with heart defects?
Background: NEC is a serious type of gut disease that can develop in infants born with cardiac defects, it causes
inflammation and injury to the gut mucosa and may be life-threatening. Making an accurate and timely diagnosis of
NEC is a significant clinical challenge. In the early stages of the disease the clinical presentation of NEC is difficult to
identify correctly. An inaccurate diagnosis of NEC results in the withdrawal of an infant's milk feed and unnecessary
administration of antibiotics, exacerbating parental stress in intensive care. Calprotectin is secreted into the lumen in
the early stages of intestinal inflammation, thus elevated faecal calprotectin concentrations are evident in infectious
and inflammatory diseases.
Aim: Assess the performance accuracy of faecal calprotectin to diagnose NEC in infants with cardiac defects
Objectives: Collect faecal samples to measure calprotectin levels before and after cardiac surgery, and at the same
time the medical team are suspicious that NEC may be developing when using curr

inconclusive suspected NEC.

Anticipated Impact

effectiveness to reduce diagnostic error - suspected NEC. Update international NEC clinical guidelines addition of

Plain English summary
Our Aims:
1. Monitor levels of a special marker found in poo before and after heart surgery to identify if this marker is higher in
infants who develop gut disease in babies born with heart defects.
2. Assess the impact of this special marker for diagnosing gut disease if added to the current tools available to
doctors.
Background
Babies born with a heart defect often require lifesaving heart surgery. A serious type of gut disease can develop in
babies born with heart defects because the heart is unable to pump enough blood to the gut, stopping it from
working correctly.
It is difficult for doctors to diagnose gut disease in the early stages due to the limitations of the current way it is
diagnosed, which includes clinical features, blood tests and gut X-rays.
This limitation in diagnosing gut disease means the medical team is unsure if gut disease is developing and
incorrectly diagnose the baby as having gut disease. This results in the baby’s milk feed being stopped for many
days and given antibiotics unnecessarily. Repeatedly stopping the baby's milk feeds leads to poor growth and
increases parents’ stress.
We want to measure the amount of a special marker

evidence.text:
found in babies' poo to see if it relates to gut disease. Adding
this special marker to the current available tests (clinical features, blood tests and gut X-rays) could improve the
accuracy of diagnosing gut disease in babies born with serious heart defects allowing for better patient care.
In our previous small study, we found a relationship between this special marker found in poo with early stages of
gut disease. However, to confirm our results, we need to measure this special marker in many more babies.
Design methods
1: Collect poo samples from babies admitted to the hospital before and after heart surgery. The collected poo
samples will be delivered to the hospital laboratory to extract and measure the amount of this special marker.
2: To assess if this special marker is higher in babies with gut disease, we will collect a poo sample at the same time
the medical team are suspicious that gut disease may be developing when using current tests available (clinical
features, blood tests and gut X-rays).
If this special marker is linked to gut disease it will help doctors to give the correct treatment to babies.

1: Collect poo samples from babies admitted to the hospital before and after heart surgery. The collected poo
samples will be delivered to the hospital laboratory to extract and measure the amount of this special marker.

the medical team are suspicious that gut disease may be developing when using current tests available (clinical
features, blood tests and gut X-rays).
If this special marker is linked to gut disease it will help doctors to give the correct treatment to babies.

Exclusion criteria
Patients will be excluded if they are older than 3 months, do not have major heart disease and do have any
gastrointestinal abnormality including gastroschisis or imperforate anus. Also, patients with neutropenia will be
excluded since calprotectin is not activated when there is neutropenia.
Data Collection
All participants will have calprotectin levels measured pre- and post-cardiac surgery. Calprotectin will also be
collected and measured if the medical team believe NEC is developing/ present using current standard assessment
(clinical assessments, laboratory tests and abdominal radiographic findings). Patients will remain in the study from
admission until discharge to home or 30 days post cardiac surgery whichever is first.
Other data collection: diagnosis (cardiac and non-cardiac conditions), interventions received by the infant (surgery
type and invasive medical treatments), cardio-pulmonary bypass time (min); weights (kg), at birth, admission, and
discharge, Z-scores for weight-for-age and length-for-age.

Study design
A prospective, multicentre, cohort-based study.
Setting
Specialist children’s hospitals undertaking cardiac surgery in England: Great Ormond Street Children’s Hospital,

Inclusion Criteria
Infants up to 3 months old, who require admission to a specialist cardiac centre for treatment of major congenital
heart disease. Require cardiac surgery or interventional catheterisation, including babies who are treated with
invasive medical therapies of respiratory support or intravenous vasoactive medications and their pathway will
include a cardiac in

, blood tests and gut X-rays).
Secondary Outcomes
 Number of days with no enteral feeds – nil by mouth (ascertained at 0600 hours).
 Number of days on 100ml/kg/hour or more of enteral feeds (ascertained at 0600 hours).
 The number of days to re-establish breastfeeding post-cardiac surgery.
 Patient length of stay in intensive care and hospital.
 Number of days on antibiotics.
 Number of days on parenteral (intravenous) nutrition.
 C-Reactive Protein (mg/l) and complete blood count.
 Formal radiological abdominal X-ray results.
 Structured clinical evaluation of the abdomen.
Feeding Protocol: patients in the study will be fed based on local unit clinical guidelines for feeding.33
REDCAP will be used to collect data at each site. Data will be anonymised to allow for data sharing.
Consent
Given that no harm is expected with participation in this observational study, a deferred consent procedure will be
used following processes used in prior PICU research.34 Patients will be enrolled on arrival at the specialist centre
soon after birth, at a time when parents have a huge amount going on. Thereafter, parents will be approached at a
suitable time when their child is stable with

written information about the study. Written informed consent will be
obtained before discharge and if any parent declines consent their wishes will be respected by removal of their
child’s data from the study.
Aim 1. Monitor faecal calprotectin levels before and after heart surgery and assess the performance accuracy of
faecal calprotectin in infants who develop NEC
Objective 1: All infants recruited to the study will have stool collection (100mg) at baseline (hospital admission), and
first stool post-surgery. Stool samples will be collected and stored in plastic test tube containers and directly sent to
hospital clinical laboratory. The calprotectin will be measured using the commercially available enzyme-linked
immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit of Bühlmann Laboratories AG (Switzerland) according to the manufacturer’s
instructions. In brief, monoclonal capture antibodies highly specific to the calprotectin heterodimeric and polymeric
complex are coated on the microliter plate when calprotectin was assayed in a single measurement using DS2
automated ELISA processing system (extended range 30–1,800 μg/g).

complications associated with cardiac problems.

nutrition and growth with overall wellbeing in relation to cardiac health and convalescents, and all had experience of

implications their child’s cardiac defect had on their gut.

define calprotectin cut off levels that are clinically significant and sensitive to distinguish between NEC and
No NEC.
 Assess the impact of the addition of a faecal calprotectin cut-off value to the current gold standard in
diagnosing or ‘ruling out’ NEC in infants who were classified as suspected/ inconclusive NEC, to measure
the patient burden of an erroneous diagnosis (nil by mouth/ antibiotic administration/ length of hospital stay)
Anticipated Impact
 Short term: Although infants and clinical staff will not experience a direct benefit or impact at this stage of the
study, we feel by including this non-invasive intervention to the diagnostic discussion for NEC will continue to
build an understanding of the biomarker and start to change the narrative around the clinical practice (stool
collection) in infants with suspected NEC, which will support the validation follow on study
 Long term: Clinical impact and patient benefit potentially within 3 years. A follow-on study (outlined in more
detail below in the future research section) to implement ‘clinical calprotectin cut off levels for cardiac NEC’
to assess the diagnostic accuracy of calprotectin when ad

ded to current diagnostic algorithm (Gold Standard
- clinical presentation and abdominal X-ray) will mitigate an inconclusive suspected NEC diagnosis,
therefore improving the timeliness and accuracy of making a NEC diagnosis.
Potential Patient Benefit:
 Avoid unnecessary withdrawal of an infant's milk feed, exacerbating parental stress in intensive care –
optimise nutritional intake.
 Improved nutritional support has been associated with increased survival and a reduction in overall morbidity
in infants with cardiac defects.
 Reduce unnecessary administration of antibiotics. Antibiotics cause antibiotic-related diarrhoea, which in turn
affects expected growth and gut dysbiosis.
 Avoid inappropriate use of intravenous nutrition as a substitute for enteral feeds during gut rest. Avoiding
intravenous nutrition reduces the risk of line sepsis
Wider health service impact
 Within 5 years: Update international clinical guidelines for the diagnosis of NEC with the addition of
calprotectin cut off levels to improve the timeliness and accuracy of a NEC diagnosis (Association for
European Paediatric and Congenital Cardiology – working group)

implications their child’s cardiac defect had on their gut.

evidence.text:
g enterocolitis and
define calprotectin cut off levels that are clinically significant and sensitive to distinguish between NEC and
No NEC.
 Assess the impact of the addition of a faecal calprotectin cut-off value to the current gold standard in
diagnosing or ‘ruling out’ NEC in infants who were classified as suspected/ inconclusive NEC, to measure
the patient burden of an erroneous diagnosis (nil by mouth/ antibiotic administration/ length of hospital stay)
Anticipated Impact
 Short term: Although infants and clinical staff will not experience a direct benefit or impact at this stage of the
study, we feel by including this non-invasive intervention to the diagnostic discussion for NEC will continue to
build an understanding of the biomarker and start to change the narrative around the clinical practice (stool
collection) in infants with suspected NEC, which will support the validation follow on study
 Long term: Clinical impact and patient benefit potentially within 3 years. A follow-on study (outlined in more
detail below in the future research section) to implement ‘clinical calprotectin cut off levels for cardiac NEC’
to assess the diagnostic accuracy of calprotectin when ad

ded to current diagnostic algorithm (Gold Standard
- clinical presentation and abdominal X-ray) will mitigate an inconclusive suspected NEC diagnosis,
therefore improving the timeliness and accuracy of making a NEC diagnosis.
Potential Patient Benefit:
 Avoid unnecessary withdrawal of an infant's milk feed, exacerbating parental stress in intensive care –
optimise nutritional intake.
 Improved nutritional support has been associated with increased survival and a reduction in overall morbidity
in infants with cardiac defects.
 Reduce unnecessary administration of antibiotics. Antibiotics cause antibiotic-related diarrhoea, which in turn
affects expected growth and gut dysbiosis.
 Avoid inappropriate use of intravenous nutrition as a substitute for enteral feeds during gut rest. Avoiding
intravenous nutrition reduces the risk of line sepsis
Wider health service impact
 Within 5 years: Update international clinical guidelines for the diagnosis of NEC with the addition of
calprotectin cut off levels to improve the timeliness and accuracy of a NEC diagnosis (Association for
European Paediatric and Congenital Cardiology – working group)
